audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Micah household shrine, silver image, ephod, teraphim, and Levite priest narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for stolen silver and maternal curse, YHWH-dedication attached to image-making, household shrine objects, appointment of a son and then a Levite as priest, Bethlehem-Judah Levite identity, purchased religious service, father-title language, and the repeated no-king/right-in-own-eyes frame.

verses.restored: There was a man from hill country of Ephraim, and his name was Micah.

He said to his mother, The eleven hundred silver taken from you, about which you uttered a curse and spoke it in my hearing—look, the silver is with me; I took it. His mother said, Blessed be my son by YHWH.

He returned the eleven hundred silver to his mother. His mother said, Consecrating, I consecrate the silver from my hand to YHWH for my son, to make a carved image and a cast image; now I return it to you.
He returned the silver to his mother, and his mother took two hundred silver and gave it to the metalworker. He made it into a carved image and a cast image, and it was in house of Micah.
The man Micah had a house of gods. He made an ephod and teraphim and filled hand of one of his sons, and he became his priest.
In those days there was no king in Israel; each one did what was right in one's own eyes.
There was a young man from Bethlehem in Judah, from clan of Judah; he was a Levite, and he was sojourning there.
The man went from city, from Bethlehem in Judah, to sojourn wherever he might find a place. He came to hill country of Ephraim, to house of Micah, while making his way.
Micah said to him, From where do you come? He said to him, I am a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah, and I am going to sojourn wherever I may find a place.
Micah said to him, Dwell with me and be to me a father and a priest, and I will give you ten silver for the days of a year, an arrangement of garments, and your food. The Levite went in.
The Levite was willing to dwell with the man, and the young man became to him like one of his sons.
Micah filled hand of the Levite, and the young man became his priest and was in house of Micah.
Micah said, Now I know that YHWH will do good to me, because the Levite has become my priest.

translation_notes: Micah confesses taking his mother's silver only after hearing her curse. Her immediate blessing appears to reverse or neutralize the curse, showing religion already entangled with fear, property, and household power.

The mother explicitly dedicates the silver to YHWH while commissioning a carved and cast image. The narrative preserves religious mixture and contradiction; it does not authorize persecution, destruction of another community's sacred objects, or contempt for living traditions.
Only two hundred of the eleven hundred silver is said to go to the metalworker. The relation between carved image and cast image, and the location of the remaining silver, require later audit.

Filling the hand is an installation or ordination idiom. Micah first installs a son and later a Levite, revealing private control over priestly office rather than a neutral model of spiritual authority.

The young Levite is economically precarious and seeking somewhere to live. His acceptance of wages, clothing, food, and a father-title must be read within material dependency, not used to shame poor clergy or workers.

Micah treats Levite identity as a guarantee of Divine favor. The chapter exposes spiritual credentialism and purchased legitimacy; it does not teach that clerical status makes a household morally safe.
The refrain about no king and doing what is right in one's own eyes is a literary judgment on social disorder. It does not prove that monarchy, authoritarian rule, or domination is the cure.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 17 to justify religious persecution, icon-smashing, antisemitism, anti-Catholic polemic, or contempt for household spiritual practice. Do not romanticize purchased clergy, dependency-based spiritual authority, or claims that status guarantees blessing. The chapter portrays a household building certainty from money, sacred objects, and a vulnerable religious worker; it does not present coercive authority as faithfulness.
